text.content: 1.0  Opening Frame  [s1_0_opening_frame]

rich_text.plain_text: 1.0  Opening Frame  [s1_0_opening_frame]

text.content: "You learned to choose scales. You checked if the biggest number fits. You checked the last digits. Now let's connect what you discovered."

rich_text.plain_text: "You learned to choose scales. You checked if the biggest number fits. You checked the last digits. Now let's connect what you discovered."

text.content: 1.1  Decision Checklist Consolidation Type B  [s1_1_decision_checklist_consolidation_type_b]

rich_text.plain_text: 1.1  Decision Checklist Consolidation Type B  [s1_1_decision_checklist_consolidation_type_b]

text.content: "Here's the first question you learned: Does the biggest number fit?"

rich_text.plain_text: "Here's the first question you learned: Does the biggest number fit?"

text.content: "Dan scored 80. That's too big for Scale of 5. You'd need a bigger scale."

rich_text.plain_text: "Dan scored 80. That's too big for Scale of 5. You'd need a bigger scale."

text.content: ⏭️ Student presses Next

rich_text.plain_text: ⏭️ Student presses Next

text.content: "Here's the second question: Do all the numbers end in 0 or 5?"

rich_text.plain_text: "Here's the second question: Do all the numbers end in 0 or 5?"

text.content: "7, 12, 19, 23. None of them end in 0 or 5. Scale of 2 is the only one that works exactly. When you look at new data, which question do you check first?"

rich_text.plain_text: "7, 12, 19, 23. None of them end in 0 or 5. Scale of 2 is the only one that works exactly. When you look at new data, which question do you check first?"

text.content:
tool: multiple_choice  options: ["Does the biggest number fit?", "Do the numbers end in 0 or 5?"]
"Which question should you check FIRST?"

rich_text.plain_text:
tool: multiple_choice  options: ["Does the biggest number fit?", "Do the numbers end in 0 or 5?"]
"Which question should you check FIRST?"

text.content: ✅ Student selected Does the biggest number fit?, correct

rich_text.plain_text: ✅ Student selected Does the biggest number fit?, correct

text.content: "Right. Check if it fits first. That tells you which scales are even possible. Then check the digits."

rich_text.plain_text: "Right. Check if it fits first. That tells you which scales are even possible. Then check the digits."

text.content: ❌ Student selected Do the numbers end in 0 or 5?, which is the second check, not the first

rich_text.plain_text: ❌ Student selected Do the numbers end in 0 or 5?, which is the second check, not the first

text.content: "That's the second question. But if you check the digits first and the biggest number doesn't fit, the scale won't work no matter what. Which question tells you if the scale is even possible?"

rich_text.plain_text: "That's the second question. But if you check the digits first and the biggest number doesn't fit, the scale won't work no matter what. Which question tells you if the scale is even possible?"

text.content: ❌ Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

rich_text.plain_text: ❌ Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

text.content: "Let me show you how to think about this. The first question is always Does the biggest number fit? If the biggest number is too big for the scale, nothing else matters. The scale can't work. So you check that first. Then, if it fits, you check the digits to see if it works exactly. The answer is Does the biggest number fit?"

rich_text.plain_text: "Let me show you how to think about this. The first question is always Does the biggest number fit? If the biggest number is too big for the scale, nothing else matters. The scale can't work. So you check that first. Then, if it fits, you check the digits to see if it works exactly. The answer is Does the biggest number fit?"

text.content: "Here's how to think about it. All four graphs show the same data: 20, 40, and 60. Graph D uses scale of 10. That means only three tick marks between 0 and 60. Fewer lines means faster reading. When your data lands on multiples of 10, scale of 10 is the easiest to read."

rich_text.plain_text: "Here's how to think about it. All four graphs show the same data: 20, 40, and 60. Graph D uses scale of 10. That means only three tick marks between 0 and 60. Fewer lines means faster reading. When your data lands on multiples of 10, scale of 10 is the easiest to read."

text.content: "Scale of 1 and 2 are for smaller data. They show every number exactly. Scale of 5 and 10 are for bigger data. They're cleaner and easier to read."

rich_text.plain_text: "Scale of 1 and 2 are for smaller data. They show every number exactly. Scale of 5 and 10 are for bigger data. They're cleaner and easier to read."
